title: TopologicalOrder

tex_statement.html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. A topological order of $G$ is a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$ such that for all $v,w \in G$: \[ v \in \Pa^G(w) \; \implies \; v < w.\] Equivalently, it can be described as an indexing of the nodes $J \cup V = \{v_1,\dots,v_K\}$ where parents always precede their children.

lean.name: IsTopologicalOrder
lean.code:
/-- `IsTopologicalOrder G r` -- the relation `r : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí Prop` is a
*topological order* of the CDMG `G`: restricted to the nodes
`G.J ‚ą™ G.V` of `G`, the relation `r` is a strict total order
(irreflexive, transitive, trichotomous), and every parent precedes
its children (`v ‚ąą Pa^G(w) ‚Üí r v w`). The behaviour of `r` on
vertices outside `G.J ‚ą™ G.V` is left unconstrained. Mirrors
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` def 3.8 verbatim. -/
structure IsTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG őĪ) (r : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí Prop) : Prop where
  /-- `r` is irreflexive on the nodes of `G`. -/
  irrefl : ‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G, ¬¨ r v v
  /-- `r` is transitive on the nodes of `G`. -/
  trans : ‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G, ‚ąÄ w ‚ąą G, ‚ąÄ x ‚ąą G, r v w ‚Üí r w x ‚Üí r v x
  /-- `r` is trichotomous on the nodes of `G`: for any two nodes
  `v, w ‚ąą G`, at least one of `r v w`, `v = w`, `r w v` holds.
  Combined with `irrefl` and `trans`, "exactly one" is automatic
  (see the design-choice block above). -/
  trichotomous : ‚ąÄ v ‚ąą G, ‚ąÄ w ‚ąą G, r v w ‚ą® v = w ‚ą® r w v
  /-- Every parent precedes its children under `r`. The LN's
  `v ‚ąą \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w`. The `v ‚ąą G` / `w ‚ąą G` hypotheses
  the LN's "for all $v, w \in G$" prose suggests are automatic from
  `v ‚ąą Pa G w`: see `mem_Pa` (which gives `v ‚ąą G`) and
  `G.E_subset` (which gives `w ‚ąą G.V ‚äÜ G`). -/
  parent_lt : ‚ąÄ {v w : őĪ}, v ‚ąą Pa G w ‚Üí r v w

lean.name: HasTopologicalOrder
lean.code:
/-- `HasTopologicalOrder G` -- the existential statement "*there is*
a topological order of `G`". The right-hand side of the iff in
claim_3_2 (`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`, Lem after
def_3_8): "$G$ is acyclic iff $G$ has a topological order". -/
def HasTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG őĪ) : Prop :=
  ‚ąÉ r : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí Prop, IsTopologicalOrder G r

lean_explanation:
The encoding takes a CDMG `G : CDMG őĪ` (a Conditional Directed Mixed Graph over an ambient vertex type `őĪ : Type*`) and, for `IsTopologicalOrder G r`, a candidate relation `r : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí Prop` whose role is the LN's strict total order $<$. The variable `r` plays the role of $<$ from the LN; the symbol is renamed because Lean reserves `<` for typeclass-resolved orders, and pushing the topological order through `LT` would force the whole carrier `őĪ` to acquire a global order. The type parameter `őĪ` is the same vertex type that everything else in the file (`Pa G w`, `v ‚ąą G`, walks, ...) reads, matching the LN's silent assumption that $G$ lives on some vertex set.

The `IsTopologicalOrder` structure bundles four `Prop`-valued fields, in one-to-one correspondence with the LN's strict-total-order axioms plus the parent-respecting clause. The fields `irrefl`, `trans`, and `trichotomous` are the three strict-total-order axioms, each guarded by `v ‚ąą G` / `w ‚ąą G` / `x ‚ąą G` hypotheses so the order constrains only `G`'s own nodes ‚ÄĒ outside `G.J ‚ą™ G.V`, `r` is free. The field `parent_lt` is the LN's $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ verbatim; it uses Lean's strict-implicit `{v w : őĪ}` binders so that `hr.parent_lt h` works without naming `v` and `w` at every call site. The `v ‚ąą Pa G w` premise already carries `v ‚ąą G` (via `mem_Pa`) and `w ‚ąą G.V ‚äÜ G` (via `G.E_subset`), so no extra membership hypotheses are needed on this field.

`HasTopologicalOrder G` is the one-line existential closure `‚ąÉ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r`, matching the LN's separate prose reading "*$G$ has a topological order*" that appears as the right-hand side of claim_3_2's acyclicity iff.
design_choices:
**Relation `r : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí Prop` on the full vertex type, not a `LinearOrder` on the subtype `‚Ü•(G.J ‚ą™ G.V)`.** The LN writes $v < w$ for bare vertices $v, w \in J \cup V$, so the topological order should accept the same vertex type that everything else in the formalisation (`Pa G w`, `v ‚ąą G`, `‚ü∂[G]`, walks, ...) reads. Carrying the relation as `őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí Prop` and constraining it *only on nodes of `G`* is the cheapest encoding that supports both formal reasoning and the LN's bare-vertex notation $v_1 < v_2 < v_3$ from the later id-algorithm chapter at the same time. The natural-looking alternative ‚ÄĒ a `LinearOrder` on the subtype `‚Ü•(G.J ‚ą™ G.V)` ‚ÄĒ would force every downstream $v < w$ statement to first inject `v` and `w` into the subtype, breaking that prose.

**Conditions restricted to `v ‚ąą G`, not a blanket strict total order on all of `őĪ`.** The LN says "*a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$*" ‚ÄĒ only the nodes of `G` are ordered. A blanket order on the whole carrier `őĪ` would be strictly stronger: it would force `r` to trichotomise non-graph vertices, which the LN does not require, and it would prevent extending a topological order arbitrarily outside `G.J ‚ą™ G.V`. The restricted form is also the natural shape for downstream reuse: a topological order of `G` is reused on the intervened graph $G_{\doit(W)}$ (claim_3_3), whose node set is the same but whose external behaviour is irrelevant.

**`structure ... : Prop` with named fields, not a four-way `‚ąß`-conjunction.** At four conjuncts, named projections start to pay off: downstream proofs reach for `hr.parent_lt`, `hr.trans`, `hr.irrefl` by name rather than peeling apart a deeply nested `And.intro`. The structure form also mirrors Mathlib's `IsTrans` / `IsTrichotomous` style of single-field property classes and keeps the LN-aligned `parent_lt` name visible at every use site.

**Trichotomy stated as "at least one of", not "exactly one".** The field reads `r v w ‚ą® v = w ‚ą® r w v`, matching `Std.Trichotomous`. The LN's "total order" reading is the exactly-one version, but together with `irrefl` and `trans` (both also restricted to `G`) the exactly-one form is automatic: `r v w ‚ąß v = w` substituted gives `r v v`, contradicting `irrefl`; `r v w ‚ąß r w v` with both endpoints in `G` gives `r v v` by `trans`, again contradicting `irrefl`. Stating only "at least one" keeps the structure lightweight without losing information.

**`parent_lt` takes `v ‚ąą Pa G w` directly, with no auxiliary `v ‚ąą G` / `w ‚ąą G` hypotheses.** Both memberships are already implicit in `v ‚ąą Pa G w`: by `mem_Pa` it unfolds to `v ‚ąą G ‚ąß v ‚ü∂[G] w`, and `G.E_subset : G.E ‚äÜ (J ‚ą™ V) √óňĘ V` then forces `w ‚ąą G.V ‚äÜ G`. The trimmed form matches the LN literally ($v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$) and saves a hypothesis at every call site.

**No `IsStrictTotalOrder` typeclass from Mathlib.** Mathlib's `IsStrictTotalOrder őĪ r` is a strict total order on *all* of `őĪ`, not on a subset. Forcing the encoding into it would require either fixing `őĪ = ‚Ü•(G.J ‚ą™ G.V)` (a subtype, see the first bullet) or extending `r` to the whole carrier (extra structure not in the LN). Spelling out the three strict-total-order axioms inline ‚ÄĒ restricted to nodes of `G` ‚ÄĒ is shorter and stays closer to the LN.

**`HasTopologicalOrder G` pulled out as its own `def`, not inlined as `‚ąÉ r, ...`.** Claim_3_2's iff and every later "let `G` be a CADMG with a fixed topological order" statement want a one-word spelling for the existential; introducing `HasTopologicalOrder` gives both readings (relation-level `IsTopologicalOrder G r` and existential `HasTopologicalOrder G`) one canonical name each and lets `simp` / `obtain` destructuring work against a single identifier. The relation-level form is still the right shape when the LN names the order, e.g. "let $<$ be a topological order" in the id-algorithm chapter.

**No separate `IsTopologicalIndexing` predicate for the LN's "indexing" reading.** The LN's "*equivalently, an indexing $v_1, \dots, v_K$*" is the same notion restated in the finite case: the choice of a bijection `Fin K ‚Üí őĪ` whose image is `G.J ‚ą™ G.V` and whose inherited `(¬∑ < ¬∑)` agrees with the topological order. It is not formalised separately because (a) the LN treats the two descriptions as interchangeable, not as distinct definitions; (b) doing so would commit the encoding to finiteness of `G.J ‚ą™ G.V`, which the underlying CDMG type does not require (the vertex type `őĪ` is fully polymorphic); and (c) downstream rows that genuinely need an indexing can derive it locally from `IsTopologicalOrder G r` together with a `Fintype` hypothesis via Mathlib's `Fintype.equivFin`.
